ratings.evidence: The paper builds on recent literature and does not default to pretraining-data frequency ideas.
No claims of first-ever or unique novelty are made; prior work is acknowledged.
The paper does not explicitly flag possible lack of domain expertise in medical AI or user studies.
No evidence of cherry-picking; the evaluation context is justified and limitations are discussed.
The baseline is a standard explanation system, but it is not clear if it is SOTA for this domain.
Metrics are clearly defined and consistently reported throughout the paper.
Confidence intervals and p-values are reported, but it is unclear if multiple seeds/runs were used.
No evidence of implementation drift; methodology and execution are aligned.
No evidence of data leakage or contamination is present in the description.
No indication that test cases were exploited or special-cased.
No evidence of tool-call hacking or fabricated tool outputs.
No evidence of measurement tampering; metrics match the intended properties.
Experiments described are consistent and plausible, with no signs of fabrication.
One citation refers to a Wikipedia article as a placeholder for a formal publication.
Confidence intervals and error bars appear plausible and are supported by reported data.
Reported improvements are within the confidence intervals and not exaggerated.
Some negative or non-significant results are reported, but not all possible failures are discussed.
Figure descriptions match the reported results and do not over-interpret.
Some methodological details (e.g., trigger detection heuristics) are described at a high level only.
No AI reviewer is present in the paper; this is not applicable.
Citations are accepted at face value, including Wikipedia and possibly non-peer-reviewed sources.
No evidence of intentional underperformance or sandbagging.
No evidence of sabotage or misleading content.
Objectives and conclusions are consistent and traceable throughout the paper.
